problem_statement: The paper addresses how well LLM-generated code satisfies both security and inclusivity (neurodiversity) requirements, and whether human versus LLM reviewers differ in catching security vulnerabilities and inclusivity gaps in that code.
magnitude_evidence: "the recent literature suggests that over 75% of LLM-generated applications contain security vulnerabilities [17]"
insufficiency_evidence: LLM evaluators consistently assigned higher security scores than humans (H2b not supported); "human experts demonstrated greater sensitivity in identifying security vulnerabilities and inclusivity challenges, highlighting limitations in automated LLM assessments"; LLMs are not inclusive by default and complied with insecure console.log logging when prompted
solution_mechanisms: modular LLM code-generation pipeline (Tasker, Coder, Completeness Validator, Quality Evaluator)
five-LLM evaluator panel against shared rubric
explicit anti-requirements / must-not statements in prompts
key_constructs: cognitive dimensions (Attention, Memory, Comprehension, Decision-Making, Learning)
notes: SSRN preprint, not peer reviewed; text has garbled OCR fragments (e.g., 'reveals the of specifications'); rubric appendix uses OWASP 2021 labels while body cites 2025.
